# Delay estimation in practice

## Biases I have not mentioned

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
- We have focused on censoring and truncation, but transmission data
  carry more
- Household and cluster data: **susceptible depletion** shortens the
  observed generation and serial intervals as susceptibles run out
- **Who infected whom** is uncertain; coprimary and tertiary cases
  contaminate pairs
- The reference cohort matters: forward versus backward intervals
- Observation is often **missing not at random**: which pairs we see
  depends on the very timing we are trying to estimate
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.attribution}
[Hart et al. 2022](https://doi.org/10.7554/eLife.70767);
[Park et al. 2021](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2011548118)
:::
:::
::::

## Andes virus: Epuyén, 2018-19 — the model

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="36%"}
- First sustained person-to-person hantavirus spread: one zoonotic
  introduction, four human generations, super-spreading
  ([Martínez et al. 2020](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2009040))
- Index cases give the incubation period; sourced cases give
  source-onset to onset
- The serial interval is a **convolution** of transmission timing
  $\delta$ and the secondary incubation period — $\delta$ can be
  negative
- $R(t)$ and offspring dispersion come from the same fit
:::
::: {.column width="64%"}
![](figures/fig-andv-model.png){width="100%"}

::: {.attribution}
Model:
[Funk et al., andv-linelist-analysis](https://github.com/epiforecasts/andv-linelist-analysis);
data
[Martínez et al. 2020](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2009040)
:::
:::
::::

## Andes virus: Epuyén, 2018-19 — results

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}
- The incubation period and transmission timing $\delta$, with the
  derived generation and serial intervals
- $\delta$ sits close to source onset, so transmission is near
  presymptomatic
- The within-host approach could replace the empirical $\delta$ here
:::
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](figures/fig-andv-results.png){width="100%"}

::: {.attribution}
[Funk et al., andv-linelist-analysis](https://github.com/epiforecasts/andv-linelist-analysis);
data
[Martínez et al. 2020](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2009040)
:::
:::
::::

## Bundibugyo Ebola: Isiro, 2012 — the model

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="36%"}
- A line list, early in an outbreak — not a serial interval, but the
  same censoring problem
- The original onset-to-death estimate was a **point estimate with no
  uncertainty**
  ([Rosello et al. 2015](https://doi.org/10.7554/eLife.09015)); the
  reanalysis adds it
- Each case has **four delays**, with a per-case identity
  $D_{od} = D_{oa} + D_{ad}$
- Death versus discharge is a **competing risk**; the split is the
  case-fatality ratio
:::
::: {.column width="64%"}
![](figures/fig-bdbv-model.png){width="100%"}

::: {.attribution}
Reanalysis:
[Funk et al., bdbv-linelist-analysis](https://github.com/epiforecasts/bdbv-linelist-analysis);
data
[Rosello et al. 2015](https://doi.org/10.7554/eLife.09015)
:::
:::
::::

## Bundibugyo Ebola: Isiro, 2012 — results

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}
- The four delays fitted under double interval censoring, compared
  across lognormal, gamma, and Weibull
- Gamma fits best (lowest WAIC); dashed lines mark the prior point
  estimates
- Fitting several families and comparing is the Charniga checklist in
  action
:::
::: {.column width="60%"}
![](figures/fig-bdbv-results.png){width="100%"}

::: {.attribution}
[Funk et al., bdbv-linelist-analysis](https://github.com/epiforecasts/bdbv-linelist-analysis);
data
[Rosello et al. 2015](https://doi.org/10.7554/eLife.09015)
:::
:::
::::
